section.title: Unlawful Sexual Relations
section.sectionRationale: This is the first extended case law of the Holiness Code, addressing sexual ethics directly and grounding it not in the customs of Egypt or Canaan but in the identity of the LORD who redeemed Israel and now calls them to a different way of life.
section.thesis: God calls Israel to a sexual ethic entirely unlike Egypt's or Canaan's, prohibiting incest, adultery, child sacrifice to Molech, same sex relations, and bestiality, because the land itself cannot bear such defilement and will vomit out those who practice it.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the call to walk in God's statutes rather than the customs of Egypt and Canaan; the list of forbidden family relations; a series of further prohibited unions and acts, including child sacrifice; and the closing warning that the land itself will not tolerate such defilement.

units.label: Not the customs of Egypt or Canaan
units.note: I am the LORD your God

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Speak to the Israelites and tell them: I am the LORD your God.

You must not follow the practices of the land of Egypt, where you used to live, and you must not follow the practices of the land of Canaan, into which I am bringing you. You must not walk in their customs.
You are to practice My judgments and keep My statutes by walking in them. I am the LORD your God.
Keep My statutes and My judgments, for the man who does these things will live by them. I am the LORD.

units.label: Family relations not to be uncovered

verses.text: None of you are to approach any close relative to have sexual relations. I am the LORD.
You must not expose the {{ervah|nakedness}} of your father by having sexual relations with your mother. She is your mother; you must not have sexual relations with her.
You must not have sexual relations with your father’s wife; it would dishonor your father.
You must not have sexual relations with your sister, either your father’s daughter or your mother’s daughter, whether she was born in the same home or elsewhere.
You must not have sexual relations with your son’s daughter or your daughter’s daughter, for that would shame your family.
You must not have sexual relations with the daughter of your father’s wife, born to your father; she is your sister.
You must not have sexual relations with your father’s sister; she is your father’s close relative.
You must not have sexual relations with your mother’s sister, for she is your mother’s close relative.
You must not dishonor your father’s brother by approaching his wife to have sexual relations with her; she is your aunt.
You must not have sexual relations with your daughter-in-law. She is your son’s wife; you are not to have sexual relations with her.
You must not have sexual relations with your brother’s wife; that would shame your brother.

You must not have sexual relations with both a woman and her daughter. You are not to marry her son’s daughter or her daughter’s daughter and have sexual relations with her. They are close relatives; it is {{zimmah|depraved}}.
You must not take your wife’s sister as a rival wife and have sexual relations with her while your wife is still alive.

units.label: Further prohibitions
units.note: Molech, and every distortion of creation's order

verses.text: You must not approach a woman to have sexual relations with her during her menstrual period.
You must not lie carnally with your neighbor’s wife and thus defile yourself with her.
You must not give any of your children to be sacrificed to Molech, for you must not profane the name of your God. I am the LORD.
You must not lie with a man as with a woman; that is an {{toevah|abomination}}.
You must not lie carnally with any animal, thus defiling yourself with it; a woman must not stand before an animal to mate with it; that is a perversion.

units.label: The land itself will not bear it
units.note: a warning, not just a rule

verses.text: Do not defile yourselves by any of these practices, for by all these things the nations I am driving out before you have defiled themselves.
Even the land has become defiled, so I am punishing it for its sin, and the land will vomit out its inhabitants.
But you are to keep My statutes and ordinances, and you must not commit any of these abominations, neither your native-born nor the foreigner who lives among you.
For the men who were in the land before you committed all these abominations, and the land has become defiled.
So if you defile the land, it will vomit you out as it spewed out the nations before you.
Therefore anyone who commits any of these abominations must be {{karet|cut off}} from among his people.
You must keep My charge not to practice any of the abominable customs that were practiced before you, so that you do not defile yourselves by them. I am the LORD your God.”

ervah.gloss: Nakedness, shame, or exposed vulnerability. The recurring phrase to uncover someone's nakedness is this chapter's euphemism for sexual relations, framing each prohibited union as an exposure of shame that God's law refuses to permit.

zimmah.gloss: Depravity, lewdness, or wicked scheming. A strong term of moral condemnation applied here to marrying both a woman and her close relatives, marking the act not as a minor irregularity but as a serious moral corruption.

toevah.gloss: Abomination, something detestable. Used across the Old Testament for a wide range of practices God finds repugnant, including idolatry and dishonest business dealings, not a single narrow category, though it carries real moral weight wherever it appears.

karet.gloss: To be cut off. A severe covenant penalty, whose precise mechanism, whether divine judgment, execution, or exclusion from the community, is debated, but whose seriousness marks these violations as a fundamental breach of covenant life.

basic.context: This chapter forbids a specific list of sexual relations, incest with close relatives, adultery, child sacrifice to the god Molech, same sex relations, and bestiality, presenting them as the practices of Egypt and Canaan that Israel must not imitate.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter's moral core is treated by the New Testament as enduring, not merely old covenant ceremony; Paul cites a case like this directly in 1 Corinthians 5 and reaffirms several of these categories in Romans 1 and 1 Corinthians 6. The specific civil penalties tied to Israel's land and nationhood, spelled out further in chapter 20, are distinct from that enduring moral core, and Christians today do not enforce them as civil law. This is a chapter that needs to be handled honestly, neither softened nor weaponized.
basic.originalLanguages: The recurring phrase to uncover nakedness, ervah, frames each forbidden union as an exposure of shame. Some acts are called zimmah, depravity, or toevah, abomination, strong moral terms used elsewhere in Scripture for idolatry and injustice as well, showing these are treated as serious moral wrongs, not mere ritual irregularities.
basic.typology: The warning that the land will vomit out the defiled anticipates final judgment on unrepented sin; yet Christ Himself was cut off from the land of the living in His people's place, Isaiah 53:8, so that those who turn to Him escape the ultimate exclusion this chapter warns of.
basic.christ: Paul names sins from exactly this category among the Corinthians and then writes, 'and such were some of you, but you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ,' 1 Corinthians 6:9-11, showing that the gospel neither minimizes this chapter's seriousness nor leaves anyone without hope of full forgiveness and change.
basic.god: God's holiness includes a real, creation rooted design for sexuality that is not culturally relative, and He grieves when that design is violated because real people, and the created order itself, are wounded by it; yet He is also the God who washes, sanctifies, and justifies those who turn to Him, whatever their past.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 5:1 addresses a case resembling verse 8 directly. Romans 1:24-27 echoes this chapter's categories in describing humanity's rebellion against creation's order. Matthew 19:4-6 shows Jesus rooting marriage in Genesis 1 and 2, the same creational foundation this chapter assumes. 1 Corinthians 6:9-11 offers full forgiveness and transformation to those entangled in exactly these sins.
basic.oneStory: God's design for sexuality in creation, one man and one woman in marriage, is guarded here against every distortion, not to shame the human body but to protect what He made good. That same design is honored by Christ, whose gospel judges sin honestly, including sexual sin, while washing, forgiving, and remaking anyone who turns to Him.

context.literary: This chapter opens the specific case law of the Holiness Code, following the general call to holiness centered on the tabernacle in chapter 17, and is followed by chapter 19's broader ethical vision and chapter 20's civil penalties for many of the same offenses.
context.historical: Israel stood between two cultures, the Egypt they were leaving and the Canaan they were entering, both of which practiced the very things forbidden here, including child sacrifice to Molech, well attested in the ancient Near East; this law marked Israel's decisive break from both.
context.canonical: This chapter's moral vision is picked up and reaffirmed across Scripture, from the prophets' condemnation of Canaanite practices to Paul's letters addressing sexual ethics in the early, largely Gentile church.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To mark Israel off decisively from the sexual practices of Egypt and Canaan, grounding sexual ethics not in cultural convention but in the character and command of the LORD who redeemed them.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter prescribes actual moral instruction for Israel, rooted in the created order of Genesis 1 and 2, not a merely ceremonial or culturally bound custom; its core prohibitions are treated as universally binding and reaffirmed in the New Testament, even as the specific civil penalties attached to some of them in chapter 20 belonged to Israel's old covenant nationhood and land tenure, and are not directly enforced as civil law by the church today.

debates.question: How should Christians read Leviticus 18's sexual prohibitions today, given that some appear alongside civil penalties in chapter 20 and beside ceremonial laws elsewhere in the Torah?

views.label: Enduring moral law, not merely old covenant ceremony
views.case: Many interpreters note these prohibitions rest on creational categories, marriage, sex, and family, not on Israel's ceremonial or civil code, and that the New Testament reaffirms several of them directly, 1 Corinthians 5:1, 1 Corinthians 6:9, Romans 1:26-27, while never reaffirming the food laws; the moral core is treated as binding on all people, not Israel alone.

views.label: A case requiring careful, non selective distinction
views.case: Others caution that chapter 20 attaches a specific civil penalty structure tied to Israel's covenant nationhood and land tenure, which does not transfer to the church or civil society today in its exact form, even where the underlying moral wrong is affirmed; the church must distinguish the enduring moral principle from the old covenant's specific civil enforcement.

debates.bearing: Both views affirm the moral seriousness of these prohibitions and their New Testament reaffirmation; they differ on how directly the old covenant's civil penalties transfer to civil law today, not on whether the underlying moral norms still hold.

study.originalLanguages: The recurring euphemism to uncover nakedness, built on ervah, frames each forbidden union as an exposure of shame rather than a neutral act. Certain unions are called zimmah, depravity, and the chapter's closing summary calls these practices toevah, abomination, a strong term used elsewhere of idolatry and dishonest scales, showing Scripture treats moral corruption of many kinds with the same weighty vocabulary.

typology.type: The land vomiting out the defiled
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates final judgment on unrepented sin, while Christ Himself was cut off from the land of the living in His people's place, Isaiah 53:8, so that those in Him escape the ultimate exclusion this chapter warns of.
Christ, by contrast, is the one who covers the shame of sinners, clothing them in His own righteousness rather than exposing them, Isaiah 61:10, Galatians 3:27.

study.christ: Paul names sins resembling this chapter's list among the Corinthian believers, then writes, 'and such were some of you, but you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God,' 1 Corinthians 6:9-11. The gospel does not soften what this chapter calls serious, but it offers what Leviticus alone could not: full washing, sanctification, and a new identity in Christ for anyone who turns to Him, whatever their history.
study.god: God's holiness includes a real design for human sexuality, rooted in creation itself, not in the shifting customs of Egypt or Canaan; He is grieved when that design is violated because it wounds real people and defiles what He declared good. Yet the same God who warns that the land cannot bear such defilement is the God who, in Christ, washes and remakes those who turn from it, showing His holiness and His mercy are not in competition.

crossReferences.note: Paul addresses a case among believers resembling verse 8 of this chapter, a man with his father's wife, treating it with the same moral seriousness.
Paul describes humanity's rebellion against the created order in categories that echo this chapter's concerns.
Jesus grounds marriage in the creation account of Genesis 1 and 2, the same creational foundation this chapter assumes.
Paul names sins from this chapter's categories, then declares that those who were entangled in them can be washed, sanctified, and justified in Christ.

study.oneStory: God's design for sexuality in creation, one man and one woman joined in marriage, is guarded here against every distortion of Egypt and Canaan, not to shame the body but to protect what God made good. That same design is honored and its violations judged honestly by Christ, whose gospel offers full washing, forgiveness, and a new identity to anyone who turns to Him.

quiz.q: What reason does God give for these commands in verses 1-5?

options.text: He is the LORD their God who is bringing them out of Egypt and into Canaan, and they must not follow either land's customs.
options.note: Verses 2-4 give this reasoning, anchored in God's own identity as the LORD.

options.text: Because the surrounding nations were militarily weaker than Israel.
options.note: This is not the stated reason; the text grounds the commands in covenant relationship with the LORD, not military comparison.

options.text: No reason at all is given for these commands.
options.note: Verses 1-5 explicitly state the reasoning, repeated as 'I am the LORD.'

options.text: Because Moses personally disliked these practices.
options.note: The text attributes these commands to the LORD speaking to Moses, not to Moses' personal preference.

quiz.q: What phrase is repeated throughout this chapter to anchor its commands?

options.text: "I am the LORD," tying every command back to God's own identity and authority.
options.note: This phrase recurs at verses 2, 4, 5, 6, 21, and 30.

options.text: "As your ancestors commanded."
options.note: The text grounds these laws in God's own word, not ancestral tradition.

options.text: "For the sake of Egypt's approval."
options.note: The chapter explicitly rejects Egypt's customs rather than seeking its approval.

options.text: No repeated phrase occurs in the chapter.
options.note: "I am the LORD" recurs multiple times through the chapter.

quiz.q: According to verse 21, what specific practice involving children is forbidden here?

options.text: Giving one's children to be sacrificed to Molech.
options.note: Verse 21 names this practice explicitly and calls it a profaning of God's name.

options.text: Sending children to be educated in Egypt.
options.note: The verse specifically addresses child sacrifice to Molech, not education.

options.text: Selling children into indentured labor.
options.note: That subject is addressed elsewhere in the law; verse 21 specifically concerns sacrifice to Molech.

options.text: No mention of children occurs in this chapter.
options.note: Verse 21 explicitly addresses this practice.

quiz.q: What warning does God give about the land itself in verses 24-28?

options.text: The land will vomit out its inhabitants if they defile it, just as it did the nations before them.
options.note: Verses 25 and 28 state this warning directly.

options.text: The land would become more fertile the more these practices continued.
options.note: The text states the opposite: the land becomes defiled and expels those who defile it.

options.text: The land's fate has no connection to Israel's conduct.
options.note: Verses 24-28 explicitly connect the land's fate to the conduct of its inhabitants.

options.text: No warning about the land is given.
options.note: Verses 25 and 28 explicitly warn about the land.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament handle the reality of sexual sin this chapter addresses?

options.text: It names such sin honestly while declaring that those entangled in it can be washed, sanctified, and justified in Christ.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 6:9-11 does exactly this.

options.text: It avoids the subject entirely.
options.note: Paul addresses these categories directly in Romans 1, 1 Corinthians 5, and 1 Corinthians 6.

options.text: It treats such sin as permanently unforgivable.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 6:11 explicitly offers forgiveness, washing, and a new identity in Christ.

options.text: There is no New Testament connection to this subject.
options.note: Multiple New Testament passages engage this chapter's concerns directly.

questions: This chapter grounds sexual ethics not in cultural convention but in the character of the LORD Himself. Where do you unconsciously let surrounding culture, rather than Scripture, set your convictions on matters of sex, marriage, or identity?

God warns that certain practices defile not only the sinner but even the land itself. How seriously do you take the idea that sin has effects reaching beyond yourself, into your family, community, or church?

Paul tells the Corinthians, some of whom had lived in exactly the sins this chapter names, that they were washed, sanctified, and justified. Is there shame from your own past, sexual or otherwise, that you have not yet let the gospel actually reach?

This chapter calls Israel to be different from both the culture they came from and the culture they were entering. Where is God calling you to a costly difference from the culture around you right now?
How can you hold together, without flinching from either side, the honesty of this chapter about sin's seriousness and the mercy of Christ toward anyone who turns to Him?

translationNotes.note: Close relative renders a Hebrew phrase literally meaning near of flesh, sharing the same root sense that runs through the following list's repeated formula of uncovering nakedness.

Molech was a deity associated with child sacrifice in the ancient Near East; some scholars debate whether every instance in the Old Testament refers to literal immolation or, in some contexts, dedication, but Leviticus 20:2-5 makes clear this practice incurred the death penalty as a capital offense.

The phrase rendered abomination, toevah, is used across the Old Testament for a range of practices, including idolatry and dishonest business dealings, and always carries strong moral disapproval rather than mere ritual irregularity.
